text:
`who` (`src/cli/mcp/tools.rs:32-48`) sends caller-identity fields — `pty_session`, `harness`/`watch_pid`, `agent`, `group`, `cwd` — all read from **environment variables**, not a config file (`src/cli/context.rs:15-47`). The daemon-side handler `rpc_who` (`src/daemon/server/who.rs:39-84`) uses these to resolve a live `Session` via `CallerAnchor` resolution. [...] User: with no agent context it should create an agent context for that session, ideally showing it as "chatgpt/echo123" for chatgpt or "grok/echo123" for grok -- I think we can associate, at least for chatgpt, based on session-id x-openai header or something like that [...] Assistant: Good signal: the MCP `initialize` handshake already receives `clientInfo` (name/version) from the connecting client and even logs it (`src/cli/mcp/access_log.rs:31-32`) — but it's discarded, not threaded through to session resolution. That's a more reliable identity source than sniffing HTTP headers, since header-sniffing (`x-openai-...`) only works in `--http` mode; the default stdio transport (which is how most harnesses spawn this) has no headers at all, only inherited env vars.

My recommendation: capture `clientInfo.name` at `initialize` time, stash it against the connection/session in the daemon, and have `rpc_who`'s no-anchor fallback auto-provision a session (`<clientInfo.name>/<random-suffix>`, matching the existing `agent/session-fragment` convention already visible in the fabric — e.g. `@codex/te-18c0e7f875d4bb90-3`) instead of collapsing to an anonymous empty-string snapshot.

Main tradeoff: this turns `who` from a pure read into something with a side effect (creates a session record on first call), and `clientInfo.name` is self-reported by the client so it's identity-by-assertion, not verified — fine for display/awareness purposes, not for anything security-sensitive. Also need to decide persistence: keyed per MCP connection (dies with the process) vs. something stabler if the same external session reconnects.
